# Proposal entries moved out: Inflection Deviation

*Generated by `scripts/27_prune_inflection_from_proposals.py`.* Agent proposals for words later triaged as Inflection Deviation (see `rare_word_inflection_only.md`) — the verses keep their original KJV wording, so these proposals are moot but preserved here with provenance; never deleted.

## accounts → reckoning — Daniel 6:2
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Per the 2026-07-16 owner ruling, the flagged rare word must be replaced with a genuinely different word, not "account" (too close a re-inflection of "accounts"). CUR's "commandments" inverted the sense (the princes render account TO the presidents, not commandments to them); "reckoning" is a period-authentic KJV word for the same relation (compare 2 Kings 22:7, "neither was reckoning made with them"), and Wycliffe's "yelde resoun to hem" and BSB's "were accountable" both confirm the underlying sense of an owed reckoning.
- NEW: And over these three presidents; of whom Daniel was first: that the princes might give reckoning unto them, and the king should have no damage.

## accuseth → chargeth — John 5:45
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "Chargeth" is a common period word for "accuseth" (compare Genesis-era legal usage), keeps the required -eth inflection, and does no violence to the sense — Moses lays a charge, i.e. accuses. Witnesses (Tyndale, Geneva, DRC, ASV) retain "accuseth" itself, confirming the meaning CUR preserves.
- NEW: Do not think that I will accuse you to the Father: there is one that chargeth you, even Moses, in whom ye trust.

## accusing → condemning — Romans 2:15
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "Condemning" reads naturally as the counterpart to "excusing," matching the sense every witness gives ("accusing or excusing/defending"), and needs no further revision.
- NEW: Which shew the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, and their thoughts the mean while condemning or else excusing one another;)

## acknowledgeth → confesseth — I John 2:23
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: DRC, ASV, Darby, and BSB all render this verb "confesseth," a precise and more common synonym that fits the -eth inflection exactly.
- NEW: Whosoever denieth the Son, the same hath not the Father: [but] he that confesseth the Son hath the Father also.

## adders → asps' — Psalms 140:3
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Per the 2026-07-16 owner ruling, "adders'" must go, not merely have its corrupted punctuation repaired. "Asps'" is a genuinely different word and is doubly period-authentic: it is CUR's own underlying attempt (now cleaned of the garbled duplicate quote mark), and the identical phrase "the poison of asps is under their lips" already stands in the KJV corpus at Romans 3:13, which quotes this very psalm — so the substitution is internally attested by the base text itself.
- NEW: They have sharpened their tongues like a serpent; asps' poison is under their lips. Selah.

## adjured → charged — Joshua 6:26
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*
- verdict: KEEP
- rationale: "Charged" is common and precisely matches RWebster's rendering; Geneva's "sware" and ASV's "charged them with an oath" confirm the sense of a solemn binding pronouncement.
- NEW: And Joshua charged them at that time, saying, Cursed be the man before the Lord, that riseth up and buildeth this city Jericho: he shall lay the foundation thereof in his firstborn, and in his youngest son shall he set up the gates of it.

## adjured → charged — I Samuel 14:24
*(from batch_0001_proposals.md)*

- NEW: And the men of Israel were distressed that day: for Saul had charged the people, saying, Cursed be the man that eateth any food until evening, that I may be avenged on mine enemies. So none of the people tasted any food.

## advised → gave counsel — Acts 27:12
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*

## affectioned → kind — Romans 12:10
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "kindly inclined" leaves the adverb "kindly" awkwardly doubled against "inclined." Copying Tyndale's construction directly ("Be kynde one to another with brotherly loue") and using the extremely common KJV word "kind" (45x) produces cleaner, more authentic phrasing per rule 5.
- NEW: Be kind one to another with brotherly love; in honour preferring one another;

## affections → passions — Romans 1:26
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: CUR's "vile sufferings" is semantically wrong — the verse names lusts God gave the Gentiles up to, not sufferings inflicted on them. "Passions" is attested KJV usage (Acts 14:15, "men of like passions") and matches ASV/ACV/BSB directly.
- NEW: For this cause God gave them up unto vile passions: for even their women did change the natural use into that which is against nature:

## affections → passions — Galatians 5:24
*(from batch_0002_proposals.md)*
- verdict: REVISE
- rationale: Same error as Romans 1:26 above — "sufferings" is semantically wrong for what the flesh is crucified with. "Passions" (attested, Acts 14:15) matches ASV/ACV/BSB and preserves the parallel "passions and lusts" construction.
- NEW: And they that are Christ's have crucified the flesh with the passions and lusts.

- NEW: He shall put on the holy linen coat, and he shall have the linen breeches upon his flesh, and shall be girded with a linen girdle, and with the linen mitre shall he be covered: these are holy garments; therefore shall he wash his flesh in water, and so put them on.

## baptisms → washings — Hebrews 6:2

- verdict: KEEP

## baptizest → NO-SAFE-SWAP — John 1:25
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- rationale: "Baptize" is a technical rite-name with no true Early Modern English synonym; every witness (Wycliffe, Tyndale, Geneva1599, DRC) retains some form of "baptize" itself. CUR's "baptizedest" is invalid on two counts — it is a mere re-inflection of the same lemma (forbidden under the owner ruling) and it wrongly shifts the verse from present into past tense, breaking the sense of an ongoing question ("Why baptizest thou then" = why do you baptize at all). No genuinely different, period-authentic word names the same rite.
- NEW: And they asked him, and said unto him, Why baptizest thou then, if thou be not that Christ, nor Elias, neither that prophet?

## baptizeth → NO-SAFE-SWAP — John 1:33
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP
- rationale: Same reasoning as John 1:25. CUR's "baptizedeth" is not even a valid English inflection and is a forbidden re-inflection of the same word besides. No period-authentic synonym for the rite exists in the witness corpus, which uniformly retains "baptizeth"/"baptiseth."
- NEW: And I knew him not: but he that sent me to baptize with water, the same said unto me, Upon whom thou shalt see the Spirit descending, and remaining on him, the same is he which baptizeth with the Holy Ghost.

## baptizeth → NO-SAFE-SWAP — John 3:26
*(from batch_0007_proposals.md)*
- verdict: NO-SAFE-SWAP

- NEW: And they came unto John, and said unto him, Rabbi, he that was with thee beyond Jordan, to whom thou barest witness, behold, the same baptizeth, and all men come to him.

## begettest → engenderest — Isaiah 45:10

Flagging lower confidence: this reading has no local corpus attestation and should be weighed by the owner-review workflow before being treated as settled.

- NEW: For in very deed, as the Lord God of Israel liveth, which hath kept me back from doing thee evil, except thou hadst hasted and come to meet me, surely there had not been left unto Nabal by the morning light any that pisseth against the wall.

"counteth" (3 occurrences) preserves the accounting sense and is used consistently for "imputeth" at Romans 4:6 in this same batch.

Darby's "this his power is become his god" and BSB's "their own strength is their god" show the clause is better recast without a participle at all, using thoroughly common words ("make," "his power," "his god").
